_instructions.description: This file contains all articles for the Simply Nerdy website.
_instructions.howToAdd: To add a new article, copy the template below and add it to the 'posts' array. Posts are displayed in reverse chronological order (newest first).

template.title: Your Article Title
template.category: One of: Games, Books, Movies, TV Shows, Music
template.excerpt: A brief 1-2 sentence summary that appears on article cards
template.content:
Full HTML content with

paragraphs,

headings,
  • lists, emphasis, bold, etc.
template.author: Simply Nerdy
template.image: Path to featured image in /images/articles/ folder (e.g., '/images/articles/article-name.jpg')

contentTips: Use <h4> for main section headings within the article
Use <em> for italic text and <strong> for bold
Keep the content engaging and conversational
Images should be 16:9 aspect ratio, ideally 1600x900px or 1920x1080px
Place article images in the /images/articles/ folder with descriptive filenames
_instructions.tagGuidelines: Use 3-5 relevant tags per post. Tags help with search and discovery.

posts.title: Nine Souls Review: The Metroidvania You Need to Play
posts.excerpt: Nine Souls is one of the best Metroidvanias ever made—a stunning marriage of Hollow Knight exploration and Sekiro combat wrapped in a story that genuinely earns its emotional weight.
posts.content:
Last year gave us some truly great games. And yet the one that captured me most wasn’t a blockbuster or even a well-known indie. It was Nine Souls—an action Metroidvania from Red Candle Games that somehow flew under almost everyone’s radar.

The game has sold over eight hundred thousand copies across all platforms. Shuhei Yoshida named it his personal game of the year for 2024. And still, barely anyone seems to know it exists. That ends here.

What Is Nine Souls?

The easiest pitch: take Hollow Knight, give it Sekiro’s combat, add a story-focused structure, a talkative protagonist, and some genuine darkness—and you’re close. Red Candle Games previously made only horror titles, and that experience bleeds into this one in the best possible way.

The game follows Yi, a protagonist who is very much not silent. Coming from Metroidvanias where the hero rarely speaks, the sheer volume of dialogue was initially surprising. But the writing earns it. Characters feel natural, conversations feel purposeful, and the story—which draws on Chinese and Taiwanese mythology with themes rooted in Taoism—ends up being one of the most memorable parts of the entire experience. More than once, it tugged at my heartstrings in ways I did not expect from this genre.

Hollow Knight Meets Sekiro

The combat is where Nine Souls truly shines. It is fast, punishing, and deeply satisfying once it clicks. Everything is built around the parry. Dashing exists, but it has a cooldown—you cannot simply dodge your way through encounters. You either learn to parry or you die, repeatedly, until you do.

The parry system has two tiers: a perfect parry that deals with incoming damage cleanly, and an imperfect parry that lets some through but leaves a recoverable health bar in its place. Get enough breathing room and your health starts coming back. Take a clean hit and that window closes. The rhythm of managing that internal health bar while staying aggressive is endlessly compelling.

Parrying also charges your talisman attack—a dash through enemies that leaves an explosive marker behind. It can’t be used without charges, which means the game is constantly rewarding aggressive, well-timed play.

The bosses are the real secret sauce. Each one demands more than the last, and the difficulty is genuinely Sekiro-level. There is a story mode with damage sliders if you need it, and there’s no shame in using it—the narrative alone is worth experiencing. But for those who want the full combat experience, the payoff of finally mastering a boss you’ve been stuck on is extraordinary.

A World Worth Getting Lost In

Nine Souls is more linear than Hollow Knight, and if you go in expecting the same level of open-ended exploration, you may need to recalibrate. The structure is tighter, the story more guided. That said, the world itself is rich enough that exploration never feels pointless—and some story moments are tied directly to what you find off the beaten path.

The art style is unlike anything else in the genre. Red Candle Games coined the term “Dao Punk” for it: traditional Taoist architecture and mysticism colliding with cyberpunk technology. Guards are cyborgs wielding swords and bows. Yi hacks machinery with talismans. Manga panels punctuate the story’s most important beats. It is visually distinctive in a way that is impossible to mistake for anything else.

A Soundtrack That Earns Its Own Review

The music combines traditional Eastern instrumentation with Western sounds in ways that feel genuinely experimental. Boss battles, quiet exploration, tense corridors, and emotional story moments all have themes that fit them perfectly. The full soundtrack is seventy-two songs and over three hours of music. It is available for ten dollars on Steam. That is an absurd deal.

Which Platform Should You Play It On?

Nine Souls is available on PlayStation 4 and 5, Xbox Series X and S, Switch 1 and 2, and PC via Steam—where it also runs on Steam Deck. PC is the optimal experience, with the game hitting up to 240fps on capable hardware and running well even on modest setups.

The Switch 1 version is unfortunately the weakest. Frame rate issues are noticeable in several areas, and at least one encounter brings performance down severely. If you have access to another platform, use it. The Switch 2 version, however, runs nearly flawlessly—and if Switch is your only option, this is the version to get.

Final Score

9.5/10

The combat alone would have been enough. Instead, Nine Souls delivers an excellent story, stunning art, and one of the best video game soundtracks in recent memory. It is a reminder that games can be more than entertainment—they can be genuine artistic expression. Do not sleep on this one.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Mario Kart World Review: Nintendo Wasn't Supposed to Make It This Good
posts.excerpt: Mario Kart World flips the formula on its head with an open world, thrilling new mechanics, and a soundtrack that hits hard on nostalgia—the best entry in the series, and it's not particularly close.
posts.content:
Nintendo wasn’t supposed to make Mario Kart World this good. And yet here we are.

The Switch 2’s marquee racing title doesn’t just iterate on what came before—it reinvents the structure of Mario Kart in ways that feel genuinely fresh, while somehow making the whole thing feel more fun, more fair, and more alive than ever.

An Open World That Actually Works

The biggest change in Mario Kart World is the seamlessly connected open world, and the skeptics were wrong about it. Rather than loading into isolated tracks, races flow directly from one course into the next across a continuous map. The stretches between courses aren’t dead air—they’re legitimate racing sections in their own right, full of shortcuts, secrets, and chaos with twenty-four players on screen at once.

The Knockout Tour mode pushes this further, stringing together routes that ignore roads entirely and send you off cliffs, through fields, and into territory the game never explicitly tells you to explore. It’s the best way to experience just how much thought went into the layout of this world.

The rewind feature—borrowed from the Forza Horizon playbook—deserves special mention. Missed a medallion? Overshot a shortcut? Rewind fixes it instantly, and it turns out it has some clever defensive applications in races too.

New Mechanics That Change Everything

Wall riding, rail grinding, charge jumping, and wave racing replace the divisive anti-gravity mechanic from Mario Kart 8, and the swap is an unambiguous upgrade. Each new ability opens up genuine skill expression—expert players are already pulling off shortcuts that look physically impossible, and even casual players will find themselves discovering new tricks deep into their playtime.

The physics of getting hit have been tuned as well. Collisions slow you down without stopping you cold. Lakitu rescues happen faster. The game respects your time in ways the previous entry often didn’t.

Coins are no longer an afterthought either. Collecting them actively increases your top speed, making coin management a real strategic consideration rather than background noise.

The Blue Shell Problem Is (Mostly) Solved

Mario Kart 8 had a rubber-banding problem. World feels markedly more random—in the good way. Skillful play is more consistently rewarded, and there are now multiple ways to counter the dreaded blue shell, including some that require timing and positioning rather than just luck. The panel spent an embarrassingly long time discussing this, and we stand by every second of it.

A Soundtrack Worth Talking About

The music in Mario Kart World is outstanding. Classic themes from across Nintendo’s history show up remixed with genuine care, and the nostalgia factor hits hard for anyone who grew up with these games. It’s one of those rare soundtracks where you notice it every time something new comes on.

A Minor Gripe

The character selection screen is a UX mess. Rather than choosing a character and then selecting their costume, every character-costume combination is thrown together into one long scrollable list. It’s a small complaint against a very large game—but when it takes twice as long as it should to pick your racer, you feel it.

Final Score

9/10

Mario Kart World is the best entry in the series and one of the strongest Switch 2 launch titles Nintendo could have asked for. It won over panel members who actively disliked Mario Kart 8, and kept the longtime fans thoroughly entertained. Whether it justifies the eighty-dollar price tag on its own is a fair debate—but the game itself is excellent.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: The Legend of Uh Review: A Fantasy Gem Full of Heart
posts.excerpt: Aaron N. Hall's debut fantasy adventure is a charming, surprisingly emotional romp that pokes loving fun at the genre while delivering characters you genuinely care about.
posts.content:
Sometimes the best book recommendations come from the most unexpected places. For us, it was a chance encounter at a convention with the author himself. One short conversation, one impulse purchase, and one very pleasant surprise later—here we are.

The Legend of Uh by Aaron N. Hall is a humorous, satirical fantasy adventure that lovingly skewers the tropes of the genre while quietly delivering something with real emotional weight. It is funnier than you expect, sweeter than you expect, and by the end, more moving than you expect.

A Quest Worth Going On

The story follows Sir DJ—a young man knighted not through any particular merit of his own, but because his beloved father, the legendary Sir Dashing, couldn’t be knighted twice. Determined to prove himself worthy of the title, DJ sets off to obtain a sacred artifact, accompanied by a crew of companions that are as charming as they are unconventional.

If you’ve spent any time with Lord of the Rings, Dungeons & Dragons, or classic fantasy video games, you’ll find plenty to recognize here. The book wears its references proudly, and the humor lands because it comes from a place of genuine love for the source material rather than mockery. There are even a few nods to well-known fantasy authors that will make readers of the genre grin.

Characters That Earn Your Investment

What elevates The Legend of Uh above a simple parody is how much it makes you care about its cast. DJ is an endearing protagonist—self-doubting, earnest, and easy to root for. His companions each bring something distinct to the group dynamic, and the relationships that develop between them across the journey feel genuinely earned rather than convenient.

One character in particular—an orc who defies every expectation the genre has set for his kind—was an immediate panel favorite. His presence alone is worth the price of admission.

More Heart Than You’d Expect

This is where the book genuinely surprised us. What starts as a lighthearted satirical romp gradually reveals a story with real emotional depth—particularly in how it handles the relationship between DJ and his father. It’s warm, specific, and quietly powerful in a way that feels personal rather than performed.

More than one of us teared up. We’re not ashamed of it.

The author’s note at the end of the book adds an extra layer of meaning that recontextualizes much of what you’ve just read. Read it. Don’t skip it.

A Few Minor Bumps

The book isn’t without its rough edges. There are occasional grammatical hiccups that briefly pull you out of the story, and a handful of scenes that feel slightly longer than they need to be. For a debut indie novel, these are easy to forgive—and they do nothing to diminish the experience as a whole.

The Audiobook

If you have access to Spotify Premium, the audiobook is absolutely worth experiencing. The narrator brings each character to life with distinct voices and personality that add a whole new dimension to the story. The orc’s voice in particular is an absolute treat.

Final Score

8.8/10

The Legend of Uh is a delightful, big-hearted fantasy adventure that earns its laughs and its tears in equal measure. Whether you’re a lifelong fantasy nerd or just looking for something charming and fun, this one is well worth your time. And based on what the author has hinted—a sequel is likely coming. We’ll be first in line.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Mission: Impossible – The Final Reckoning Review: The Mission Was Simply Possible
posts.excerpt: Tom Cruise defies age and logic once again in a spectacular action spectacle—though the sky-high stakes of The Final Reckoning occasionally work against the tension it's reaching for.
posts.content:
Tom Cruise is sixty-three years old. He is doing his own stunts. He will not stop. And honestly? Good.

Mission: Impossible – The Final Reckoning is the eighth entry in a franchise that somehow keeps finding new ways to put its star in genuinely dangerous situations, and it delivers exactly what that promise implies: breathtaking practical action, a likable ensemble, and at least one sequence that belongs among the best the series has ever produced.

Practical Effects in an Age of CGI

The most consistent thing Mission: Impossible has going for it is also its most valuable: a commitment to real stunts, real locations, and real danger. In an era where most action films are assembled in post-production, watching Cruise hang off planes, dive underwater, and sprint through set pieces knowing it’s actually him on screen carries a weight that no amount of visual effects can replicate.

That commitment pays off in a major way here. There’s one extended sequence in particular that our panel agreed is not just a series highlight—it’s a genuine standout moment for the action genre as a whole. The tension is real, the stakes feel physical, and the execution is remarkable on a technical level.

Big Stakes, Familiar Formula

Where the film loses some ground is in the size of its own ambitions. The threat at the center of The Final Reckoning—a rogue AI with the power to trigger global catastrophe—is so enormous that it paradoxically deflates some of the tension. When the stakes are literally the entire world, it becomes difficult to genuinely believe the worst could happen. The series has always asked you to suspend disbelief, but here the formula is stretched thin enough that a few of us found ourselves comfortable in our seats when we probably should have been on the edge of them.

The AI villain itself is an intriguing concept that doesn’t quite deliver on its potential. The idea of an entity that can simulate every possible outcome is compelling—and timely—but the rules around what it can and can’t do feel fuzzy in ways that occasionally undercut the logic the film is trying to build.

The Ensemble Makes It Work

What saves the film emotionally is the people around Ethan Hunt. The supporting cast has always been one of this franchise’s quiet strengths, and The Final Reckoning leans into those relationships in ways that land hard. Several character moments hit with genuine emotional weight, and at least one scene involving a long-running cast member had the whole panel feeling it.

The humor lands too. Simon Pegg remains a delight, and the film knows when to let the cast breathe between the chaos.

A Satisfying Conclusion—Mostly

Whether this truly feels like a finale is a question our panel split on. The action delivers. The emotional beats mostly earn their place. But the ending leaves enough threads open that calling this a definitive conclusion feels optimistic at best—and knowing Tom Cruise, probably intentional.

If this is the last one, it’s a respectable send-off. If it isn’t, well—we called it.

Final Score

7.6/10

The Final Reckoning is exactly what a Mission: Impossible film should be—spectacular, relentless, and anchored by a star who seems constitutionally incapable of slowing down. It’s not the tightest entry in the series, and a few of its biggest swings don’t quite connect. But when it works, it really works. Best experienced loud, big, and ideally in the best seat in the house.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Superman (2025) Review: Is This the Best Superman Movie?
posts.excerpt: James Gunn's Superman reboot is charming, surprisingly emotional, and anchored by a villain performance for the ages—a genuinely great superhero film that earns its hope.
posts.content:
Nobody walked into James Gunn’s Superman expecting it to be anyone’s favorite Superman film. Several of us walked out feeling exactly that way.

This is the first entry in Gunn’s new DC Universe, and it sets the tone with confidence: lighter in spirit than the Snyder era, warmer in character, and genuinely fun to watch from start to finish. It’s not a perfect film—but it’s a very good one.

A Superman Worth Rooting For

The most important thing a Superman movie can do is make you want to cheer for him. This one does that effortlessly.

David Corenswet steps into the role and makes it his own. His Superman is hopeful, likable, and grounded in a way that feels true to the character’s comic book roots. Where the Snyder-era Superman was deliberately stoic and isolated by design, Gunn’s version leans into the warmth that has always defined the character at his best.

The film also solves one of superhero cinema’s oldest problems: how do you create real stakes for an invincible hero? The answer here is clever, character-driven, and results in a third act payoff that genuinely earns its emotional landing.

The Best Lex Luthor in Years

If there’s a single element of this film that stands above the rest, it’s the villain.

This Lex Luthor is obsessive, petty, terrifyingly smart, and completely unhinged in the most entertaining way possible. The performance is magnetic, the motivations are crystal clear, and the character delivers at least one line in the final act that had the whole panel talking. He’s a genuinely great movie villain—the kind that makes the hero’s eventual triumph feel earned rather than inevitable.

Gunn’s Signature Style

Anyone familiar with Gunn’s work on Guardians of the Galaxy or The Suicide Squad will recognize his fingerprints immediately. The humor is sharp and character-driven. The tone is lighter without being weightless. The ensemble cast of supporting heroes each get moments to shine, with a Green Lantern that surprised us most.

Some viewers have pushed back on the film’s lighter tone, but it’s worth remembering that Gunn helped pioneer this style of superhero storytelling in the first place. Applied to Superman—a character who has always been defined by hope rather than darkness—it fits.

The soundtrack deserves a mention too. It’s genuinely great.

A Few Minor Complaints

Not everything lands perfectly. A few of the supporting heroes feel underdeveloped—present in the story but not yet given room to breathe. Some character moments in the third act could have been pushed a little further to deepen the emotional impact. And the film wears its universe-building ambitions on its sleeve in ways that occasionally pull focus from the story at hand.

None of it is fatal. But there’s a sense that some threads are being held for later installments rather than fully explored here.

Final Score

8.4/10

Superman is a joyful, well-crafted superhero film that reminds you why the character has endured for nearly a century. It finds the emotional core of Clark Kent, pairs him with one of the best screen villains in recent DC history, and sends you out of the theater with a smile. One of us called it the best Superman film yet. We’re not going to argue.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Donkey Kong Bananza Review: Hold My Banana
posts.excerpt: Donkey Kong Bananza is a joyful, destructive masterpiece—a love letter to franchise fans and a stunning showcase for the Switch 2 that absolutely nobody should sleep on.
posts.content:
Nobody asked for a new Donkey Kong game to be this good. And yet here we are.

Donkey Kong Bananza arrives as one of the Switch 2’s marquee titles, and it delivers in ways that surprised even the skeptics on our panel. This is a game that takes a simple, satisfying idea and builds an entire world around it—then keeps finding new ways to make that world feel fresh.

Destroy Everything

The centerpiece of Bananza is its fully destructible environments, and the first thing to know is that it feels as good as it looks. Punching through walls, tearing chunks of terrain apart, riding pieces of the world like a surfboard, hurling debris at enemies—it never gets old. The particle effects when you smash through gold in particular are pure eye candy.

What makes it work beyond the novelty is how much freedom it gives you. Like Breath of the Wild or Tears of the Kingdom, there’s almost always more than one way to accomplish any given task. Players who want to dig straight through a mountain can. Players who want to find the clever environmental solution can do that too. The game accounts for both approaches and rewards curiosity across the board.

Bananza Transformations

Scattered throughout the game are Bananza transformations that give DK entirely new abilities and movesets. Each one changes how you interact with the world in meaningful ways, and the musical themes that accompany each form are an absolute highlight—the kind of tracks that start off seeming a little cheesy and end up lodged in your brain permanently.

The variety keeps the gameplay feeling fresh from start to finish, and discovering new transformations is one of the game’s most consistently satisfying moments.

A Surprisingly Heartfelt Story

Nobody walked into Donkey Kong Bananza expecting to be emotionally moved. Several of us were anyway.

The inclusion of Pauline as DK’s companion gives the game a genuine emotional core, and the relationship between the two characters is handled with more warmth and care than you’d expect from a Nintendo platformer. Music plays a meaningful role in the story as well—not just as background ambience, but as an actual narrative element woven into the plot.

The Mario Odyssey team is two for two. This feels every bit as lovingly crafted as that game, just with a very different energy.

Fan Service Done Right

For longtime Donkey Kong fans, Bananza is packed with rewards. Classic enemies return with their original sound effects intact. Certain levels pull directly from the visual and musical language of the SNES trilogy. Hidden details throughout the game suggest developers who grew up loving this franchise and wanted to share that love with the people playing it.

None of it feels cynical or obligatory. It feels earned.

A Few Minor Gripes

No game is perfect. Some panel members noted occasional frame rate dips during especially intense moments—understandable given how much the game is rendering at any given time, but noticeable. Camera angles can get awkward in tight spaces. And while the combat arenas are fun in small doses, they show up frequently enough that a few players found them a bit repetitive compared to the more inventive exploration sections.

These are small complaints against a very large canvas. Nothing here significantly undermines the experience.

Final Score

9/10

Donkey Kong Bananza is a joyful, endlessly inventive platformer with a surprising amount of heart. It respects where the franchise came from while building something genuinely new, and it sticks its landing in spectacular fashion. If you have a Switch 2, this one is not optional.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Split Fiction Review: A Rift in the Industry
posts.excerpt: Hazelight Studios does it again—Split Fiction is a wildly creative, endlessly surprising co-op experience that proves genuine two-player games are far from a dying art.
posts.content:
In an era of live service games, battle passes, and four-player online everything, Split Fiction feels like a breath of fresh air.

Hazelight Studios has quietly carved out a niche that almost no one else occupies: tight, story-driven co-op games designed exclusively for two players. No solo mode. No extra players. Just two people, working together, sharing a screen. And with Split Fiction, they’ve delivered their best work yet.

Built for Two—And Only Two

The thing that makes Split Fiction special starts before you even boot it up. This is a game designed from the ground up around the co-op experience. Neither player is ever just along for the ride. At any given moment, one of you might be controlling the environment while the other navigates through it, or solving one half of a puzzle while your partner handles the other. The roles are always different, always essential, and always interesting.

It’s genuine co-op in a way that’s genuinely rare—and it makes a difference you feel immediately.

What makes it even better? Only one player needs to buy the game. The other can download a free friend pass and jump in at no cost. From EA, of all publishers. The gaming world continues to surprise.

Endless Creative Variety

If there’s one word to describe Split Fiction’s level design, it’s relentless. The game throws new mechanics, new settings, and new ideas at you constantly—and almost never repeats itself. What starts as one kind of experience transforms into something completely different around every corner.

Some stretches feel like tributes to beloved games and genres, lovingly recreated through Hazelight’s co-op lens. Others go completely off the rails in the most delightful ways. There are moments that had our panel laughing out loud, moments that genuinely impressed with their visual ambition, and moments that quietly wrecked us emotionally.

The game is a jack of all trades—and proudly so. It doesn’t try to outdo dedicated genre titles at their own game. It borrows from everywhere, puts its own spin on it, and makes the variety itself the point.

A Story Worth Playing Through

The narrative follows two writers with clashing personalities—one drawn to science fiction, the other to fantasy—who find themselves trapped together inside a machine that brings their stories to life. The setup is clever, and the story earns its emotional moments without forcing them.

Character development is a genuine strength here. Both leads start at a distance and grow into something more meaningful across the course of the game, and their individual backstories are handled with more maturity and care than you might expect from a co-op action game. More than one panel member mentioned that the story moved them to tears—and that doesn’t happen often.

The pacing also benefits from optional side stories scattered throughout each chapter—short, bite-sized adventures that serve as a fun change of pace without interrupting the main narrative flow.

A Few Minor Gripes

No game is perfect. Some players may find that the platforming starts to feel familiar after a while—gaps to jump, hooks to grapple, the same basic motions repeated across very different-looking environments. For a game this varied in concept, the underlying mechanics occasionally show their seams.

It’s a small complaint, though, and one that most players will barely notice in the moment. When the environments are this inventive and the story this engaging, the fundamentals mostly fade into the background.

Final Score

9.6/10

Split Fiction is exactly the kind of game the industry needs more of—creative, generous, and genuinely built around shared experience. Whether you play it with a partner, a sibling, or a friend from across the hall, it’s one of the most purely enjoyable co-op experiences in years. Don’t miss it.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Wicked Review: Defying Gravity and Mostly Sticking the Landing
posts.excerpt: The Wicked two-parter delivers stunning visuals, immaculate vocals, and a story worth telling—even if Part Two loses some of the magic that made Part One soar.
posts.content:
Few adaptations carry as much expectation as Wicked. The musical has been a cultural institution for decades, and bringing it to the big screen meant satisfying die-hard theater fans while also introducing the story to a whole new audience.

For the most part, it delivers.

Wicked Part One — A Delightful Surprise

For anyone coming in without a deep familiarity with the source material, Part One is a genuine revelation. The story is charming, the world-building is rich, and the production design is nothing short of spectacular. Sets are constructed with real ambition, costumes pop with color, and the choreography finds creative ways to use every inch of space the screen allows.

But the undeniable centerpiece is the vocal performances. Cynthia Erivo and Ariana Grande both bring extraordinary talent to their roles. Erivo in particular commands every scene she’s in—her voice carries a clarity and power that makes the big musical moments feel genuinely earned. Grande, who had some skeptics going in, proves more than capable, bringing charm and personality to Glinda that feels entirely her own.

The film is warm, whimsical, and full of energy. It captures the fun of musical theater while adding a cinematic scale that the stage simply can’t replicate. If you’re new to the story, Part One is an outstanding introduction. If you know the show well, there’s still plenty here to enjoy—including some thoughtful additions that deepen the world in ways the stage production never could.

Part One Score: 9.5/10

Wicked For Good — A Darker Turn

Part Two is a noticeably different film in tone and feel. Where Part One leaned into the whimsy and fun of musical theater, For Good goes darker—and it makes no apologies for it. The stakes are higher, the story is heavier, and some of the lightness that made the first film so winning is simply gone.

That shift will work for some viewers and not for others. Those who love a dark, emotionally complex story will find a lot to appreciate. Those expecting the same breezy energy of Part One may find themselves caught off guard.

The vocal performances remain a consistent strength. Erivo continues to deliver, and certain musical moments in For Good hit with a genuine emotional gut-punch that surpasses anything in the first film. When the tears come, they’re earned.

Where the film struggles is in sustaining the charm that made the Glinda and Elphaba dynamic so compelling in Part One. The odd-couple friendship that drives the entire story feels less balanced here, and some viewers may find the relationship harder to connect with as a result. It’s a noticeable shift, and not everyone will agree on whether it serves the characters or undercuts them.

The film also stretches a three-hour stage production across two features, which means some story beats arrive quickly while others are given room to breathe that they may not entirely need. The pacing is uneven, though the payoffs—when they land—are genuinely impressive.

Part Two Score: 8/10

The Verdict

Wicked as a two-part cinematic event is ambitious, visually stunning, and anchored by vocal performances that are as good as anything the musical genre has produced on screen. Part One is the stronger film—lighter, more fun, and more consistently satisfying. Part Two earns its emotional moments but loses some of what made the first half so special.

If you’ve never experienced the story of Elphaba and Glinda, this is a worthwhile way to meet them. And if you’re a longtime fan of the stage production, there’s enough here—and enough that’s genuinely new—to make both films worth your time.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: The Archaeoptimist Review: Spock's Beard Still Has It
posts.excerpt: Spock's Beard delivers one of the standout prog albums of the year—layered, lyrically rich, and anchored by a 21-minute epic that alone is worth the price of admission.
posts.content:
It's been a while since Spock's Beard put out a new album, and The Archaeoptimist was absolutely worth the wait.

Produced by keyboardist Ryo Okumoto—who also co-wrote nearly the entire record alongside Michael Whiteman—this is a band firing on all cylinders with a fresh lineup that still honors everything that made them great.

A Strong Opening

The album opens with Invisible, one of the early singles, and it sets the tone immediately. It's catchy, almost poppy by prog standards, and earwormy in the best way. A highlight is a synth section around the three-and-a-half-minute mark that feels like flying through a retro space shooter—it's weird, it's fun, and it's very Spock's Beard.

The a cappella harmonies scattered throughout the album are a recurring strength. The band has always had an incredible blend, and they lean into it here in ways that genuinely impress.

Growing Pains and Hidden Depth

Electric Monk is the one track that takes the most time to win you over. On first listen, some moments feel a little heavy-handed. But revisiting it with the lyrics in front of you adds significant context, and what initially felt off starts to click. It's a grower.

4 Thoughts—the fourth installment of the band's beloved “Thoughts” series—is a standout. It pays homage to earlier entries in the series through callbacks and polyphonic counterpoint, while still carving out its own identity. Lyrically, it pulls off something genuinely clever: the chorus subtly shifts its wording across the album to reframe its entire meaning, moving from a sense of malicious guidance to one of compassion. It's the kind of lyrical precision that separates good prog from great prog.

Saint Jerome in the Wilderness

This one rewards patience and a little research. The song draws from the history of Leonardo da Vinci's unfinished painting of the same name—a work that was literally cut into five pieces after his death before being painstakingly restored. That historical detail becomes a launching point for something much more ambitious: a meditation on fragmentation, identity, and what it means to value art as purpose rather than commodity.

It also isn't hard to read the song as a reflection on Spock's Beard's own history—years of lineup changes, shifting leadership, and a band reassembling its creative identity piece by piece. Whether intentional or not, it lands with weight.

Musically, a piano and drum section around the three-minute mark completely changes the texture of the song, dropping into a jazzy, almost cinematic stretch that comes out of nowhere and absolutely delivers.

The Archaeoptimist — The Main Event

The title track is a 21-minute, seven-part suite—and it's the best thing on the album.

The concept is built around a fascinating word: an archaeologist explores the past, while an optimist looks toward the future. The Archaeoptimist blends both, telling a post-cataclysmic story of a father and daughter learning to survive and lead in a broken world. It's a story about history, power, hope, and what we owe each other when everything familiar is gone.

The suite's central argument is quietly profound: learning history without wisdom is meaningless, and power justified by fear is destined to repeat the old world's mistakes. The ending delivers a payoff that earns every one of its twenty-one minutes.

Musically, the suite is a feast. Groovy bass-driven sections, Stranger Things-style eighties synths, jazzy interludes, and moments that call to mind Final Fantasy soundtracks all coexist within a single, coherent piece. New drummer Nick Potters shines throughout, bringing real dynamism to some of the suite's most adventurous stretches.

Closing Strong

The Next Step closes the album on a high note. Built around a gorgeous opening piano motif, it's a song about choosing forward motion before stagnation sets in—embracing fear as part of progress rather than a reason to stop. It's one of the most emotionally resonant tracks on the record, and its final moments leave you genuinely wondering what chapter comes next for this band.

Final Score

8.8/10

The Archaeoptimist is a worthy addition to Spock's Beard's catalog and one of the standout prog releases of the year. It balances the band's classic DNA with a fresh creative voice, delivers some of the most thoughtful lyrics they've written in years, and builds to a title track epic that alone justifies the listen. If you've never given Spock's Beard a shot, this is a very good place to start.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Wind and Truth Review: Practically Perfect in Every Way
posts.excerpt: Brandon Sanderson closes out the first arc of The Stormlight Archive with an emotionally devastating, cosmically ambitious, and practically flawless conclusion that left us in stunned silence.
posts.content:
Wind and Truth should not be able to do what it does.

It is over thirteen hundred pages long. It ends a story arc nearly fifteen years in the making. It carries the weight of five interconnected books, a sprawling cast, and an entire shared universe. And somehow, Brandon Sanderson sticks the landing—almost perfectly.

A Book-Long Sanderlanche

Longtime Sanderson fans know the Sanderlanche: that explosive final stretch where everything converges and your heart rate doubles. Wind and Truth turns that into a thirteen-hundred-page experience.

Nearly every chapter escalates. Revelations layer on top of revelations. Cosmic lore drops land consistently throughout, not just at the end. It's almost disorienting how relentless the pacing is—and that's a compliment. For a book this long to have almost no dull moments is a genuine achievement.

Criticisms of the pacing exist, but we simply don't agree. The intensity never flatlines. The average chapter hits harder than most novels' climaxes.

A Masterclass in Mental Health Storytelling

One of the strongest recurring themes across The Stormlight Archive has always been mental health—and this book is Sanderson's most ambitious exploration of it yet.

Characters who have divided readers in previous entries land very differently here. Sanderson doesn't shy away from the messy, non-linear reality of trauma and recovery. If anything, some of the most quietly powerful moments in the book come from characters simply learning to sit with their pain rather than outrun it.

It's clear the research is genuine. This doesn't feel like a writer guessing at what struggle looks like. It feels lived-in and real.

Every Character Gets Their Moment

What's remarkable about a book this size is that almost no one feels shortchanged. Each major character arc has a distinct emotional journey, and several characters who felt like supporting players in earlier books step fully into the spotlight here.

Without giving anything away: one character's backstory stands as arguably the best origin in the entire series. Another's arc is a quiet, hard-earned evolution that reflects genuine growth rather than manufactured drama. The Heralds—long portrayed as fallen and broken figures—receive a reframing that adds enormous emotional weight to everything that came before.

Redemption is the throughline. Not cheap redemption, but the kind that costs something.

Lore, Revelations, and the Bigger Picture

For Cosmere readers, this book is an absolute feast. Deep questions about the history of Roshar, the nature of the Shards, and the broader forces at work across the universe get meaningful answers here—while opening just as many new doors.

The ending is simultaneously more devastating and more hopeful than we anticipated. It doesn't wrap everything up neatly, but it earns every emotional beat it reaches for. The world of Roshar looks dramatically different by the final page, and the implications for where the story goes next are staggering.

Final Score

9.8/10

Wind and Truth is Brandon Sanderson's magnum opus. Tragic, hopeful, lore-dense, and emotionally resonant across every one of its thirteen hundred pages. Some finales disappoint. This one earns everything it does. Practically perfect in every way.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Project Hail Mary Review: The Best Sci-Fi Novel of the Decade
posts.excerpt: Andy Weir’s Project Hail Mary blends hard science, humor, and heart into an unforgettable sci-fi adventure that’s as emotionally powerful as it is intellectually thrilling.
posts.content:
Some sci-fi novels impress you with scale. Others win you over with ideas. Project Hail Mary does both—and then somehow manages to break your heart in the process.

Andy Weir’s follow-up to The Martian is bigger, bolder, and even more ambitious. It’s a story about astrophysics, extinction-level threats, interstellar travel… and friendship.

Hard Science, Made Human

Weir leans fully into hard science fiction. The calculations are detailed. The astrophysics is dense. The biology is speculative but grounded. And yet, somehow, it never feels inaccessible.

Through the perspective of Ryland Grace—a middle school science teacher turned accidental astronaut—the book explains complex concepts with clarity and humor. Even when the science gets heavy, the voice remains engaging and human.

This is Weir at his best: rigorous without being overwhelming.

A Brilliant Narrative Structure

The story unfolds through alternating timelines. Ryland wakes up alone on a spaceship with no memory of who he is or why he’s there. As his memories return, we piece together the mission alongside him.

This structure creates tension and mystery without sacrificing emotional payoff. Each revelation recontextualizes what we thought we knew, culminating in one of the most impactful twists in recent sci-fi.

And yes—the twist hits hard.

Rocky

It’s impossible to talk about Project Hail Mary without talking about Rocky.

Rocky is one of the most creative alien concepts in modern fiction. His biology, communication method, and worldview are radically different from humanity’s—and yet his friendship with Ryland becomes the emotional core of the novel.

What begins as cautious curiosity evolves into one of the best fictional bromances in recent memory.

Their collaboration isn’t just heartwarming—it’s necessary. Neither species can survive without the other. That theme of cooperation across impossible differences elevates the story beyond simple survival.

Hope in the Face of Extinction

At its core, this is an optimistic book.

Yes, the Earth is facing disaster. Yes, governments make questionable decisions. But the story ultimately argues that humanity—and even alien civilizations—can rise to extraordinary challenges when they work together.

The ending is deeply emotional without feeling manipulative. It’s earned. It’s satisfying. And it lingers.

The Audiobook Experience

If you haven’t listened to the audiobook, it’s worth experiencing at least once. The narration enhances key moments in ways that are uniquely suited to the medium.

It’s rare for an audiobook adaptation to add something meaningful to the text—but this one does.

Final Score

9.7/10

Project Hail Mary is one of the most compelling sci-fi novels of the decade. It balances scientific precision with humor, tension with hope, and spectacle with genuine emotional depth. If you love science fiction—or even if you’re just sci-fi curious—this is essential reading.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Kirby Air Riders Review: Sakurai’s Absolute Cinema
posts.excerpt: Kirby Air Riders isn’t just a sequel—it’s a spectacular celebration of the entire Kirby franchise, blending chaos, nostalgia, and cinematic ambition into a perfect package.
posts.content:
Some sequels feel inevitable. Kirby Air Riders feels impossible.

More than two decades after the original cult favorite, Masahiro Sakurai returns to Kirby’s racing spin-off and delivers something far bigger than anyone expected. This isn’t just a revival. It’s a full-blown celebration of Kirby’s legacy—and one of the most surprising 10/10 experiences we’ve had.

Joy in Motion

From the moment you boot it up, the game radiates personality. The visuals are vibrant and expressive, running buttery smooth while maintaining crystal-clear readability during chaotic races. Tracks burst with color and energy, and cinematic rail sections elevate races into spectacle.

Wave Flow Waters isn’t a calm water level—it’s a party. Galactic Nova isn’t just a backdrop—it’s a looming presence. Every course feels crafted with intention.

The presentation feels heavily inspired by Sakurai’s work on Smash Bros., but here it’s dialed toward pure Kirby joy.

Simple Controls, Deep Mastery

The one-button control scheme may sound limiting on paper, but it’s deceptively clever. Boosting, drifting, braking, and maneuvering are all built around timing and control rather than button complexity.

It’s easy to learn and surprisingly deep to master.

And then there’s City Trial.

City Trial remains one of the most chaotic, addictive multiplayer modes ever conceived. With online support and up to sixteen players, it feels like a battle royale before battle royales were mainstream. It’s unpredictable, hilarious, and endlessly replayable.

Even outside City Trial, the game thrives on unlockables. Rather than handing everything to you upfront, Air Riders embraces progression. You’re constantly chasing new vehicles, characters, challenges, and secrets. It taps into that satisfying “one more run” energy few racing games manage to capture.

Customization and Fan Service

Air Rider customizations are wildly creative, letting players showcase absurd, hilarious, or surprisingly artistic designs. It adds another layer of personality to an already vibrant package.

But the real fan service lies deeper.

An Unexpected Epic

What begins as a racing game escalates into something far more ambitious. The single-player campaign evolves into a cinematic, emotionally resonant experience packed with callbacks to Kirby’s history.

Legendary bosses return. Familiar themes are remixed with grandeur. Galactic Nova looms as an unforgettable presence. And what starts as racing gradually transforms into full-blown spectacle.

Without spoiling specifics, the final sequence doesn’t feel like the ending of a spin-off. It feels like the finale to an entire franchise era.

The scale, music, and visual direction reach heights that feel almost Blizzard-level cinematic—except this is Kirby.

It’s over-the-top. It’s emotional. It’s bold.

It’s absolute cinema.

Sakurai’s Passion Project

This game feels personal. Sakurai didn’t have to make it. Kirby Air Ride was a cult classic, not a guaranteed blockbuster. Yet Air Riders feels crafted with the care of someone closing a creative circle decades in the making.

It’s rare for a franchise to receive this kind of reverent treatment so many years later. Rarer still for it to land this successfully.

Final Score

10/10

Kirby Air Riders is chaotic, heartfelt, cinematic, and endlessly replayable. It rekindles nostalgia without relying on it, delivers spectacle without sacrificing fun, and proves that sometimes dream sequels really can happen.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Metroid Prime 4 Review: A Stunning Return With Divisive Choices
posts.excerpt: Metroid Prime 4 delivers the best visuals and combat in the series, but new design decisions and divisive changes keep it from perfection.
posts.content:
After nearly two decades of anticipation, Metroid Prime 4 is finally here. And somehow, it’s both exactly what fans wanted—and not quite what they expected.

This is easily the best-looking game Nintendo has ever produced. Running at 4K and 60 frames per second, the performance is buttery smooth, the environments are stunning, and the visor details add a level of immersion that feels next-gen. Watching Samus’s cannon freeze in cold environments or lightning crackle across metal surfaces is the kind of polish that reminds you how much time went into this project.

Combat Has Never Felt Better

If there’s one area where Metroid Prime 4 unquestionably excels, it’s combat.

The gunplay feels weightier and more dynamic than any previous entry. Missiles have real concussive force. Beam upgrades feel distinct and strategic. Encounters are more demanding, and for the first time in the series, normal mode can genuinely push players to a game over screen.

Hard Mode ramps that intensity even further, turning late-game encounters into serious tests of positioning and precision.

And then there’s mouse mode.

The addition of mouse-style aiming transforms the experience. Once adjusted, it becomes the definitive way to play. Free-aim combat shines here, especially during tougher boss fights. It feels like a glimpse into the future of the franchise.

A More Guided Metroid

Where opinions begin to split is in the game’s structure.

Unlike earlier Prime entries, this installment introduces more NPCs and direct guidance. Some players appreciate the added accessibility, while others miss the heavy isolation and environmental storytelling that defined earlier games.

The large open desert region, Soul Valley, is the most controversial addition. It acts as a hub connecting several major areas. Some see it as refreshing openness compared to narrow backtracking corridors. Others find it underdeveloped and occasionally tedious, particularly when tied to optional resource collection.

The truth likely lands somewhere in the middle: the foundation is strong, but the execution isn’t flawless.

Upgrades and Psychic Abilities

The game introduces psychic powers into Samus’s arsenal. While visually interesting, many of the core upgrades echo familiar beam types and abilities from earlier entries.

That said, their implementation feels refined. Beam effects are faster, more reactive, and more satisfying in combat. Even returning concepts benefit from modern polish.

Still, after such a long development cycle, some players may have expected more radically new mechanics layered into the formula.

Boss Battles and Challenge

Boss encounters are a highlight. The final confrontation with Sylux stands out as one of the most intense battles in the Prime series. Earlier late-game areas also introduce moments of real tension, bordering on horror.

Difficulty is another divisive topic. Some players found exploration more streamlined and puzzle design more direct. Others argue that optional challenges still provide plenty of depth for those willing to engage fully.

Where It Lands

Metroid Prime 4 is not a safe sequel—but it’s not a total reinvention either. It modernizes the formula, adds accessibility, experiments with structure, and delivers the most refined combat the series has seen.

Some design choices may frustrate longtime fans who prefer pure isolation and minimal guidance. But beneath those debates lies a technically excellent, mechanically satisfying entry in the franchise.

Final Score

8/10

Visually stunning and mechanically sharp, Metroid Prime 4 proves the series still has life—while leaving just enough room for refinement in whatever comes next.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Iron Lung Review: Cosmic Horror at Its Most Claustrophobic
posts.excerpt: Based on the indie horror game, Iron Lung delivers a grim, claustrophobic descent into cosmic dread that proves originality still thrives in horror.
posts.content:
Iron Lung is the kind of horror film that shouldn’t exist—and that’s exactly why it works.

Based on the small indie game created by David Szymanski, the film takes a minimalist concept and stretches it into a grim, atmospheric dive into cosmic and deep-sea horror. The result is one of the most unique horror experiences of the year.

A Simple but Terrifying Premise

The story begins with a chilling setup: one day, the stars and planets vanish. Humanity calls it the “Silent Rapture.” What remains are scattered survivors on space stations, low on resources and desperate for answers.

The only lead? A distant planet covered entirely in an ocean of blood.

The main character—a convict—gets welded inside a tiny submarine and lowered into that ocean. The vessel is barely large enough to stand in. The viewing windows are sealed shut to prevent implosion. Navigation relies on coordinates and grainy photographs taken with an external camera.

It’s claustrophobic. It’s isolating. And it leans fully into the fear of the unknown.

Cosmic Horror Done Right

This is Lovecraftian horror in its purest form. The terror doesn’t come from constant action or loud spectacle. It comes from implication.

The film gives you fragments—sounds outside the submarine, distorted images, unsettling clues—and trusts your imagination to fill in the gaps. When it ends, you’re left with questions. Not in a frustrating way, but in a way that lingers.

That restraint is what makes it powerful. Horror is at its strongest when it doesn’t explain everything.

Indie Spirit, Big Atmosphere

Despite its limited budget, Iron Lung succeeds because of its concept and execution. The production design is grim and oppressive. The music amplifies the dread without overwhelming it. The atmosphere never lets up.

The film is rated R for strong language and blood, and it fully commits to its bleak tone. There’s no comic relief to soften the edges. No comforting resolution waiting at the end.

Some viewers may feel the runtime could be trimmed slightly, but the slow burn adds to the suffocating tension. The pacing reinforces the isolation rather than distracting from it.

Markiplier’s Performance

Markiplier steps into the lead role and, considering this is not a traditional Hollywood production, delivers a solid performance. He’s not giving a career-defining dramatic masterclass—but he doesn’t need to. The concept carries the weight, and his performance supports it effectively.

Much like other cult horror classics that began with modest budgets, Iron Lung thrives on originality rather than star power.

Why It Stands Out

Hollywood is often criticized for recycling the same ideas. Iron Lung is the opposite of that. It’s a unique film based on a unique indie game, merging cosmic horror with deep-sea terror in a way that feels fresh.

It may not be the absolute best horror film of the year, but it might be the most original—and originality counts for a lot.

Final Score

8.5/10

Iron Lung is dark, immersive, and memorably strange. If you’re a fan of cosmic horror, claustrophobic settings, or films that trust your imagination, this is one worth seeking out.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy

posts.title: Devotion Review: The Most Disturbing Horror Game We’ve Ever Played
posts.excerpt: Red Candle Games delivers a psychological horror experience that transcends jump scares, telling a devastating story about family, culture, and misplaced faith.
posts.content:
Devotion is not just a horror game. It’s an experience that lingers long after the credits roll.

We went in blind expecting a tense psychological thriller. What we got instead was one of the most emotionally devastating stories we’ve ever experienced in gaming.

A Horror Game Without Combat

If you're expecting something like Resident Evil or Dead Space, reset your expectations. Devotion has no combat. No weapons. No resource management.

This is a first-person, story-driven psychological horror game built around exploration and environmental puzzles. The fear doesn’t come from monsters chasing you. It comes from truth slowly revealing itself.

The game unfolds inside a Taiwanese apartment across several periods of time, following a small family: a father, mother, and young daughter. What begins as subtle unease gradually escalates into dread as the family’s struggles come into focus.

Real Horror Hits Harder

What makes Devotion stand apart from other horror titles is that its terror is grounded in reality. The supernatural imagery and nightmare sequences serve as symbolism, not spectacle.

At its core, this is a story about pride, cultural pressure, mental health, and a father who desperately wants to fix his family but refuses to confront the real problem.

As financial strain grows, the father becomes obsessed with success and appearances. When his daughter begins experiencing anxiety and emotional distress, he refuses to accept psychological explanations. Instead, he turns to ritual, superstition, and eventually a cult promising spiritual healing.

The horror isn’t in a monster under the bed. It’s in watching a parent make catastrophic decisions while believing he’s doing the right thing.

Symbolism and Cultural Context

Red Candle Games doesn’t just tell a family story—it immerses you in Taiwanese culture of the 1980s, where status, reputation, and traditional expectations carry enormous weight.

The game also explores the darker side of religious extremism and cult influence. Rather than offering a simplistic critique, it presents how desperation can make anyone vulnerable to manipulation.

Every surreal sequence—whether it's distorted hallways, ritualistic imagery, or body horror—reflects the father’s internal unraveling. The apartment itself becomes a psychological maze of guilt and denial.

Moments That Stay With You

There are effective jump scares, yes. But the most powerful moments are quiet ones: a locked bathroom door, a child’s drawing, a song that takes on new meaning by the end.

The game steadily builds toward a final revelation that reframes everything. When the truth lands, it doesn’t shock for spectacle—it devastates.

Devotion understands that the most powerful horror leaves space for your imagination. It doesn’t need to show everything to make you feel it.

An Emotional Aftermath

This is a short game that can be finished in one sitting, but its impact is anything but small. It forces reflection—on parenthood, on pride, on the consequences of misplaced devotion.

By the end, we weren’t talking about puzzles or graphics. We were sitting in silence.

Final Score

9.7/10

Devotion is masterful storytelling delivered through the medium of games at its most powerful. It’s uncomfortable, haunting, and unforgettable. If you’re willing to experience horror that cuts deeper than jump scares, this is essential.
posts.author: Simply Nerdy
